title: The Confusion Matrix
concept: The Confusion Matrix is a 2x2 grid that maps a model's predictions against actual outcomes, showing exactly where it gets confused.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main goal of optimizing for high recall?

options.text: Catching as many actual positive cases as possible, minimizing missed cases
Minimizing false alarms at all costs
Ensuring the model's predictions are always exactly 100% correct

exercises.explain: Correct! High recall ensures you catch the positives (like sick patients), even if it means some false alarms.

cards.title: The boy who cried wolf
cards.body: When the boy says 'Wolf!' and there is a wolf, that's correct. When he says 'Wolf!' and there isn't one, that's a false alarm. The villagers need a grid to keep track of his guesses!

cards.title: What is a confusion matrix?
cards.body: A confusion matrix is a table layout that visualizes the performance of a classification model. It shows the count of correct and incorrect predictions, split by class.

cards.title: Sorting your files
cards.body: Imagine sorting mail into two piles: 'Keep' and 'Toss'. A confusion matrix is like checking your work and counting how many kept letters were important, and how many tossed letters were junk.

cards.title: The 2x2 grid
cards.body: The matrix has four boxes: True Positives (correctly said Yes), True Negatives (correctly said No), False Positives (said Yes, actually No), and False Negatives (said No, actually Yes).

cards.title: Myth: It's only for binary classification
cards.body: False. While a 2x2 grid is used for Yes/No decisions, the confusion matrix can expand to any number of classes, like a 3x3 grid for classifying animals (cat, dog, bird).

cards.title: The 4 quadrants
cards.body: The anatomy of a 2x2 Confusion Matrix:

exercises.prompt: In a confusion matrix, what is a False Positive?

options.text: A false alarm (the model predicted positive, but it was actually negative)
A missed case (the model predicted negative, but it was actually positive)
A correct prediction of a negative class
A prediction that was not evaluated

exercises.explain: A False Positive is a false alarm — the model incorrectly guessed positive.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A True Negative represents a correct prediction where the model correctly said 'No'.
exercises.explain: True. True Negatives are cases where the model correctly identified that the negative class was present.

exercises.sentence: If a model fails to detect a tumor, classifying a sick patient as healthy, this is a False ___.
exercises.explain: It is a False Negative because the model predicted a negative result, but the reality was positive.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a True Positive?

options.text: A security alarm goes off when an actual burglar breaks in
A smoke detector sounds when someone is cooking dinner
A medical test says you are healthy, and you are indeed healthy

exercises.explain: The model predicted a positive event (burglary/alarm) and it actually happened (burglar), which is a True Positive.

exercises.prompt: A credit card company checks 100 transactions. The confusion matrix shows: 90 True Negatives, 8 True Positives, 2 False Positives (false alarms), and 0 False Negatives (missed fraud). How did the model perform?

options.text: It performed exceptionally well, catching all fraud cases with only 2 false alarms.
It performed poorly because it missed all positive cases.
It performed poorly because accuracy was only 8%.
It is impossible to tell without knowing the recall score.

exercises.explain: Exactly! With 8 True Positives and 0 False Negatives, it caught all fraud. 2 False Positives are a tiny price to pay. 🎉
